past_medical_history: History of iron deficiency anemia, treated with oral supplementation
family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus, systemic lupus erythematosus
No family history of epilepsy or psychiatric illness

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, approximately 2-3 drinks per weekend
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a house; no children

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but confused and intermittently agitated. Oriented to self only; unable to state the month, year, or location. Speech is fluent but tangential with occasional incoherent statements and paranoid ideation, stating that hospital staff are trying to poison her. She exhibits intermittent pressured speech alternating with periods of flat affect and paucity of speech lasting 1-2 minutes. Unable to perform serial sevens. Recalls 1 of 3 objects at 5 minutes. Cannot follow two-step commands reliably.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Mild left lower facial droop noted intermittently but not consistently reproducible on repeat examination. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. Intermittent perioral dyskinesias with repetitive lip-pursing movements observed.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone mildly increased in bilateral upper extremities. Strength grossly 4+/5 in all extremities when patient cooperates, though testing is limited by fluctuating cooperation and intermittent agitation. No clear lateralizing weakness. Occasional brief episodes of bilateral upper extremity posturing noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain symmetrically in all four extremities. Detailed sensory testing unreliable due to encephalopathy and poor cooperation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk), Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses equivocal bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing attempted on right side with mild dysmetria; patient refuses to cooperate with left-sided testing. Heel-to-shin not performed due to agitation.
neurological_exam.gait: Not assessed; patient is agitated and requires soft restraints for safety.
neurological_exam.additional: Intermittent orolingual dyskinesias with lip-pursing and tongue protrusion observed, initially attributed to anxiety by outside psychiatric evaluation. One brief episode of staring with behavioral arrest lasting approximately 10 seconds witnessed during examination, with no clear post-ictal state. Heart rate fluctuated between 88 and 118 bpm during the 40-minute encounter. Diaphoresis noted intermittently.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion, insomnia, and one witnessed seizure over 3 weeks
patient.history_present_illness: This 25-year-old right-handed Hispanic woman presents to the emergency department accompanied by her husband after three weeks of progressive confusion and behavioral changes culminating in a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure earlier today. According to her husband, symptoms began approximately three weeks ago when the patient developed worsening insomnia, sleeping only 2-3 hours per night despite previously having no sleep difficulties. She became increasingly anxious, irritable, and emotionally labile, crying without provocation and expressing uncharacteristic paranoid beliefs that her coworkers were conspiring against her. She was placed on administrative leave from her teaching position after behaving erratically at school, including leaving her classroom mid-lesson and becoming verbally aggressive toward a colleague. Her husband took her to an urgent care clinic ten days ago, where she was assessed by a physician who diagnosed acute anxiety disorder and prescribed lorazepam 0.5 mg as needed. She took the lorazepam intermittently with minimal benefit. Over the past week, her husband noticed worsening memory problems: she could not recall conversations from hours earlier, repeatedly asked the same questions, and became lost driving home from the grocery store two blocks away. Four days ago, he noticed involuntary lip-pursing and tongue movements that he initially attributed to a nervous tic. Two days ago, the patient was found by her husband wandering the house at 3 AM appearing confused and unable to recognize their bedroom. This morning, she had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 70 seconds with urinary incontinence, after which she remained confused for over an hour. EMS was called and brought her to this facility. She has no prior history of seizures or psychiatric illness. There is no recent travel, no known sick contacts, no tick exposure. She has not started any new medications other than lorazepam. She denies recent headache, fever, or neck stiffness, though her husband notes she felt warm to the touch two nights ago.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly formed 6-7 Hz theta activity over the posterior regions, no clear alpha rhythm identified

findings.morphology: Semi-rhythmic, moderate to high amplitude (80-150 microvolts), intermittent runs lasting 5-15 seconds

findings.frequency: Evolving rhythmic theta-delta at 3-4 Hz building to 6-7 Hz over 30 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic sharp-contoured waveforms with gradual frequency evolution
findings.state: Occurred during an observed episode of behavioral arrest and staring

artifacts.description: Intermittent EMG artifact from jaw clenching and orolingual dyskinesias obscuring temporal channels

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient inability to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating moderately disorganized background with generalized rhythmic delta activity, one electrographic seizure of left temporal origin correlating with a clinical staring episode, and intermittent frontal rhythmic delta activity. No extreme delta brush pattern is identified. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: 30-minute routine recording; continuous EEG monitoring strongly recommended to quantify seizure burden and evaluate for subclinical seizures. Muscle artifact from orolingual movements intermittently degrades temporal electrode signal quality.

findings.location: Right medial temporal lobe, involving the hippocampus and amygdala

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity, could represent artifact versus early pathology

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
Paranasal sinuses: mild mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus
mri.impression: Subtle FLAIR hyperintensity in the right medial temporal lobe without restricted diffusion, enhancement, or mass effect. No evidence of hemorrhagic encephalitis to suggest HSV. No acute ischemia, mass lesion, or hydrocephalus. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Chloride 97 mEq/L (L), Glucose 109 mg/dL (H), CRP 0.9 mg/dL (H), CK 193 U/L (H), Urine drug screen Positive for benzodiazepines  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Urine drug screen: Positive for benzodiazepines (H)

special_tests.Cytology: Scattered reactive lymphocytes; no malignant cells
special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Equivocal (2 faint bands in CSF, not clearly matched in serum)
special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Pending (sent to reference laboratory, results expected in 5-7 days)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 20 cmH2O. WBC: 12 cells/uL (mildly elevated) (7% PMN/88% lymph). Protein: 49.1 mg/dL. Glucose: 56.2 mg/dL (ratio 0.61 (serum glucose 102 mg/dL)). Gram stain: No organisms seen. HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. VZV PCR: Negative. Enterovirus PCR: Negative. Cytology: Scattered reactive lymphocytes; no malignant cells. Oligoclonal bands: Equivocal (2 faint bands in CSF, not clearly matched in serum). IgG index: 0.72 (borderline elevated, normal <0.70).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:80)  (H), CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:160)  (H).

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 3.5 x 2.8 x 2.9 cm, contains multiple peripheral follicles consistent with known PCOS. No discrete mass identified.

3.8 x 2.6 x 3.1 cm, polycystic morphology. A 1.2 cm complex echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing identified within the ovarian parenchyma, possibly representing a small dermoid or hemorrhagic cyst.
Normal size, anteverted. Endometrial stripe 8 mm, appropriate for cycle phase.
Small amount of physiologic free fluid in the cul-de-sac

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left ovary 3.8 x 2.6 x 3.1 cm, polycystic morphology. A 1.2 cm complex echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing identified within the ovarian parenchyma, possibly representing a small dermoid or hemorrhagic cyst.  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Left ovary: 3.8 x 2.6 x 3.1 cm, polycystic morphology. A 1.2 cm complex echogenic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing identified within the ovarian parenchyma, possibly representing a small dermoid or hemorrhagic cyst. (H)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor Ab (CSF) Positive (1:160)  (H), ANA 1:80 speckled  (H).

signal_characteristics.density: Heterogeneous with a small focus of fat density (-30 HU) and a punctate calcification

output.impression: No evidence of other malignancy or metastatic disease. Bilateral polycystic ovaries are noted. The teratoma, though small, should be considered as a potential immunologic trigger for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands in CSF, not present in serum)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 20 cmH2O. WBC: 31 cells/uL (elevated, increased from prior 12 cells/uL) (4% PMN/92% lymph). Protein: 67.2 mg/dL. Glucose: 54.5 mg/dL (ratio 0.60 (serum glucose 98 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands in CSF, not present in serum). IgG index: 0.81 (elevated). Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:160). Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes; no malignant cells.

background.overall_organization: Severely disorganized, worsened compared to initial routine EEG
background.continuity: Continuous but with increasing periods of voltage suppression

findings.location: Left temporal onset (T3/T5) with bilateral spread
findings.frequency: 4 electrographic seizures captured over 24-hour monitoring period
findings.morphology: Rhythmic sharp waves evolving in frequency and amplitude over 30-60 seconds
findings.state: 3 subclinical, 1 with subtle clinical correlate (behavioral arrest with oral automatisms)
findings.clinical_correlation: Significant subclinical seizure burden requiring anti-seizure medication optimization
Can be seen in encephalitis, metabolic encephalopathy, or prion disease; clinical correlation needed

findings.type: Possible evolving extreme delta brush pattern
findings.frequency: 1-2 Hz delta with intermittent superimposed 18-25 Hz beta
findings.morphology: Suggestive of but not fully developed extreme delta brush; intermittent and not sustained
findings.clinical_correlation: If confirmed, this pattern is highly suggestive of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

artifacts.description: Frequent artifact from orofacial dyskinesias and intermittent patient agitation despite sedation

output.impression: Severely abnormal 24-hour continuous EEG demonstrating worsening background disorganization, 4 electrographic seizures of left temporal origin (3 subclinical), generalized periodic discharges, and intermittent epochs suggestive of an evolving extreme delta brush pattern. The combination of these findings in a young woman with subacute encephalopathy is highly concerning for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.
output.limitations: Frequent movement artifact from dyskinesias limits interpretation of some temporal electrode data.
recommended_actions: Optimize anti-seizure medication to address subclinical seizure burden
Continue EEG monitoring to assess treatment response
Consider ICU admission if seizure burden escalates

differential.key_distinguishing: Initial psychiatric presentation with paranoia, insomnia, and behavioral changes is easily mistaken for a primary psychiatric disorder. However, the subsequent development of seizures, orolingual dyskinesias, autonomic instability, CSF pleocytosis, and abnormal EEG argue strongly against a primary psychiatric etiology.

HSV encephalitis typically presents more acutely with high fever, unilateral temporal lobe involvement with hemorrhagic changes and restricted diffusion on MRI, and positive CSF HSV PCR. This patient has a subacute course, subtle unilateral MRI findings without hemorrhage, and negative HSV PCR.

Hashimoto encephalopathy presents with elevated anti-TPO antibodies and often with thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO levels. Family history of autoimmune disease raises the question but serologies exclude this diagnosis.

Family history of SLE in the mother could raise suspicion. However, low-titer ANA (1:80) without other SLE criteria (no rash, arthritis, serositis, renal involvement, cytopenias) makes this diagnosis very unlikely. Anti-dsDNA and complement levels would be expected to be abnormal in active CNS lupus.

No exposure to antipsychotics or dopamine-blocking agents. CK elevation is mild (210 U/L) rather than the markedly elevated levels (>1000 U/L) typical of NMS. No lead-pipe rigidity or extreme hyperthermia.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: establish IV access, continuous cardiac and pulse oximetry monitoring, administer IV lorazepam 2 mg if seizure occurs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hemodynamic stabilization; seizure control if active

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs: CBC, BMP, hepatic panel, CRP, ESR, TSH, free T4, CK, ammonia, urine drug screen, blood alcohol level
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia, elevated CK, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, normal thyroid function, normal ammonia, UDS positive for prescribed benzodiazepine

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle right medial temporal FLAIR signal change; nonspecific but concerning for encephalitis versus post-ictal changes

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF sent for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, HSV/VZV/enterovirus PCR, oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, borderline protein, negative viral PCRs, equivocal oligoclonal bands, pending anti-NMDAR antibodies

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h while awaiting HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage for HSV encephalitis until PCR confirms negative

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG followed by continuous EEG monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Generalized slowing, electrographic seizure, FIRDA; later continuous monitoring reveals evolving extreme delta brush and subclinical seizures

optimal_actions.action: Initiate anti-seizure medication: levetiracetam 1000 mg IV loading dose followed by 500 mg IV q12h
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in clinical and electrographic seizure activity

optimal_actions.action: Given high clinical suspicion for autoimmune encephalitis despite pending antibodies, initiate first-line immunotherapy: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Begin immunotherapy without waiting for antibody results given strong clinical suspicion based on the clinical phenotype, EEG findings, and CSF pleocytosis

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Indeterminate left ovarian lesion requiring further characterization

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for comprehensive paraneoplastic workup and to further characterize ovarian lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Small left ovarian teratoma confirmed; no other malignancy

optimal_actions.action: Consult gynecologic oncology for teratoma resection once patient is stable
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical planning for removal of immunologic trigger

optimal_actions.action: Implement fluid restriction for mild SIADH; monitor serial sodium levels
If insufficient clinical improvement after 2 weeks of first-line immunotherapy: escalate to rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 doses as second-line immunotherapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical improvement with B-cell depletion therapy

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric IV acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Recognize that the initial psychiatric presentation does not exclude autoimmune encephalitis and pursue neurological workup
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV steroids and IVIG) without excessive delay, even before antibody results return, given strong clinical suspicion
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
Initiate anti-seizure medication for seizure management
Obtain continuous EEG monitoring to assess for subclinical seizure burden
Admit to ICU or monitored setting given autonomic instability and seizures
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder and discharging without neurological workup
Administering high-dose antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol) which can worsen dyskinesias and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Delaying lumbar puncture in the absence of contraindications
Waiting for antibody results before initiating immunotherapy when clinical suspicion is high and the patient is deteriorating
Rapid sodium correction (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome)
Brain biopsy, which is not indicated when autoimmune encephalitis can be confirmed serologically
key_reasoning_points: A young woman presenting with subacute psychiatric symptoms progressing to seizures and movement disorder should prompt consideration of autoimmune encephalitis, even when the initial presentation appears psychiatric
The initial psychiatric misdiagnosis (anxiety disorder at urgent care) is a common pitfall with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and contributes to the moderate difficulty of this case
Subtle orolingual dyskinesias may be mistaken for anxiety-related behaviors or tics, but are a hallmark feature of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
MRI is normal or shows only subtle findings in approximately 50% of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis cases; a near-normal MRI does not exclude the diagnosis
Mild CSF pleocytosis with equivocal oligoclonal bands in this case requires the clinician to maintain high suspicion and await antibody results rather than dismissing the diagnosis
The family history of autoimmune disease (maternal SLE, maternal aunt with RA) provides context for autoimmune predisposition but does not directly confirm the diagnosis
The EEG does not show classic extreme delta brush initially, requiring the clinician to correlate the non-specific findings with the clinical picture and order continuous monitoring
Immunotherapy should be initiated based on clinical suspicion and supportive findings even before confirmatory antibody results return, as delays in treatment worsen outcomes

metadata.distinguishing_features: Moderate difficulty with initial psychiatric misdiagnosis, subtle MRI findings, mild CSF abnormalities with pending antibodies, and EEG without classic extreme delta brush on initial recording
